course_info.title: Data Structures and Algorithms
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Formally analyze algorithm time and space complexity using asymptotic notation
Apply Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta notation correctly and distinguish between them
Analyze iterative algorithms by counting operations in loops and nested loops
Establish the mathematical foundation for rigorous algorithm comparison
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 1.4 - Analysis of Algorithms

lecture_notes.introduction:
Data Structures and Algorithms is the most important course in the computer science curriculum. Every technical interview, every system design decision, and every performance optimization you encounter in your career depends on the concepts you learn here. This is where you move from writing code that works to writing code that scales.

In CS101 and CS102, you informally discussed Big-O notation. Now we formalize it mathematically. Big-O gives an upper bound on growth: f(n) is O(g(n)) if there exist constants c and n0 such that f(n) <= c * g(n) for all n >= n0. Big-Omega gives a lower bound, and Big-Theta gives a tight bound (both upper and lower). These definitions are precise, and you must be able to apply them rigorously.

The ability to analyze algorithms separates engineers who guess at performance from those who predict it. When someone proposes a solution in a design meeting, you should be able to immediately estimate its time and space requirements. Is a nested loop acceptable for 1000 elements? Yes. For 10 million? Probably not — that is O(n^2) = 100 trillion operations.

This week establishes the analytical framework we will use throughout the course. Every data structure and algorithm we study will be characterized by its complexity, and you will be expected to derive that complexity from first principles, not memorize it from a table.
key_concepts: Big-O definition: f(n) = O(g(n)) if there exist constants c > 0 and n0 such that f(n) <= c*g(n) for all n >= n0
Big-Omega definition: f(n) = Omega(g(n)) if there exist constants c > 0 and n0 such that f(n) >= c*g(n) for all n >= n0
Big-Theta definition: f(n) = Theta(g(n)) if f(n) = O(g(n)) and f(n) = Omega(g(n)) — a tight bound
Common growth rates ordered: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
Analyzing loops: a single loop from 1 to n is O(n); nested loops multiply — two nested loops over n are O(n^2)
Analyzing recursive algorithms: set up a recurrence relation T(n) and solve it
The Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d) has solutions based on comparing log_b(a) to d
Amortized analysis: average cost per operation over a sequence, even when individual operations vary
Worst-case vs average-case vs best-case: we usually analyze worst-case unless stated otherwise
Space complexity: how much extra memory beyond the input does the algorithm use
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze the time complexity of five progressively complex code snippets: constant-time array access, a single loop with arithmetic, nested loops with dependent bounds, a binary search variant, and a divide-and-conquer function. For each, formally prove the Big-O bound by identifying the dominant term.
setup_requirements: IDE configured for the chosen implementation language
CLRS textbook or equivalent reference available

review.key: What is the difference between Big-O and Big-Theta?
review.value: Big-O is an upper bound (at most this fast). Big-Theta is a tight bound (exactly this fast asymptotically). Saying binary search is O(n) is technically true but imprecise; Theta(log n) is the precise characterization.

review.key: What is amortized analysis?
review.value: Some operations are expensive rarely but cheap usually. Amortized analysis averages cost over a sequence. Dynamic array append is O(1) amortized even though individual resizes are O(n), because resizes happen exponentially less frequently.

review.key: How do you analyze nested loops?
review.value: Multiply the iterations of each loop. A loop from 1 to n containing a loop from 1 to n gives n*n = O(n^2). If the inner loop depends on the outer (1 to i), sum the series: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n^2).

review.key: What does the Master Theorem give you?
review.value: For recurrences T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d), it provides the solution directly: if log_b(a) < d, T(n) = O(n^d); if equal, O(n^d log n); if greater, O(n^(log_b a)). This handles most divide-and-conquer algorithms.

class_schedule.topic: Arrays, Dynamic Arrays, and Amortized Analysis
learning_objectives: Understand the memory layout and performance characteristics of arrays
Implement a dynamic array with amortized O(1) append
Prove the amortized O(1) bound using the aggregate and accounting methods
Compare array-based implementations with linked alternatives
readings: Python Documentation: How Python lists are implemented internally

lecture_notes.introduction:
Arrays are the most fundamental data structure in computer science. They provide O(1) random access by index because elements are stored contiguously in memory — the address of element i is simply base_address + i * element_size. This property makes arrays the backbone of nearly every other data structure.

Static arrays have a fixed size determined at creation. Dynamic arrays (Python's list, Java's ArrayList, C++'s vector) overcome this limitation by automatically resizing when full. The resizing strategy is crucial: doubling the capacity when full ensures that append operations are O(1) amortized, even though individual resizes are O(n).

Amortized analysis formalizes this argument. The aggregate method sums the total cost of n operations and divides by n. The accounting method assigns each cheap operation an extra 'credit' that pays for future expensive operations. Both prove that the average cost per append is O(1), which is what matters for the overall running time of a program that does n appends.

Understanding arrays deeply — their memory layout, cache behavior, resizing cost, and comparison with linked structures — is essential because arrays underlie so many other data structures: hash tables use arrays of buckets, heaps use arrays for implicit trees, and even graphs are often stored as arrays of adjacency lists.
key_concepts: Static arrays: contiguous memory, O(1) access by index, fixed size at creation
Dynamic arrays: resize when full, typically doubling capacity to maintain O(1) amortized append
Amortized analysis (aggregate method): total cost of n appends is O(n), so average cost is O(1)
Amortized analysis (accounting method): charge each append $3 — $1 for the insert, $2 saved for future resize
Array operations: access O(1), append O(1) amortized, insert at index O(n), delete at index O(n), search O(n)
Cache locality: arrays are cache-friendly because elements are contiguous; linked lists are not
Python list internals: over-allocates by ~12.5% to reduce resize frequency
Java ArrayList vs LinkedList: ArrayList for random access, LinkedList for frequent insertion/deletion at known positions
Multi-dimensional arrays: row-major vs column-major storage affects loop performance
Tradeoffs: arrays sacrifice flexible insertion for fast access; linked lists do the opposite
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a DynamicArray class from scratch with append, get, set, insert, and delete methods. Track the number of element copies during resizes. Run 10000 appends and graph the cumulative copy count to visually demonstrate the amortized O(1) behavior. Compare timing with Python's built-in list.
setup_requirements: Understanding of Big-O from previous session
Matplotlib or terminal plotting for visualization (optional)

review.key: Why does doubling give O(1) amortized append?
review.value: After n appends, the total copy work from all resizes is 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n = 2n - 1. Dividing total work (n appends + 2n copies) by n gives O(1) per operation.

review.key: Why not increase by a constant amount instead of doubling?
review.value: Adding a constant k means resizing every k appends, each costing O(current size). Total copies become O(n^2/k) = O(n^2), making amortized append O(n). Doubling is essential for the O(1) amortized bound.

review.key: What is cache locality?
review.value: When the CPU accesses memory, it loads a block (cache line) containing nearby addresses. Arrays benefit because sequential elements are nearby. Linked list nodes are scattered, causing cache misses on nearly every access.

review.key: When would you choose a linked list over an array?
review.value: When you need frequent O(1) insertions/deletions at known positions and rarely access by index. Examples: implementing a queue (deque), LRU cache eviction, and maintaining sorted sequences with frequent updates.

class_schedule.topic: Linked Lists: Singly, Doubly, and Circular
learning_objectives: Implement singly, doubly, and circular linked lists with full operations
Solve classic linked list problems: reversal, cycle detection, merge sorted lists
Analyze the time and space complexity of all linked list operations
Use sentinel nodes and dummy heads to simplify edge case handling
readings: Cracking the Coding Interview, Ch. 2 - Linked Lists

lecture_notes.introduction:
Linked lists are the foundation of pointer-based data structures. While you implemented basic linked lists in CS102, this session pushes deeper: circular variants, sentinel nodes, and the classic algorithmic problems that interviewers love and real systems require.

A circular linked list connects the last node back to the first, eliminating the concept of 'end.' This is useful for round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, and any situation where you cycle through elements repeatedly. The implementation requires careful handling to avoid infinite loops during traversal.

Sentinel nodes (dummy head/tail nodes that contain no data) eliminate special cases in insertion and deletion. Without a sentinel, inserting at the head requires updating the head pointer separately from inserting elsewhere. With a sentinel, every insertion — including at the head — follows the same 'insert after a node' logic. This simplification reduces bugs and makes code easier to reason about.

We also study classic linked list algorithms that appear in technical interviews and real codebases: reversing a list in place (the three-pointer technique), detecting cycles (Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm), finding the middle element in one pass, and merging two sorted lists. These problems train the pointer manipulation skills needed for trees and graphs.
key_concepts: Singly linked list: each node has data and next; head pointer; O(1) prepend, O(n) append without tail pointer
Doubly linked list: each node has data, next, and prev; O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions
Circular linked list: last node points back to first; no None terminator; requires careful traversal logic
Sentinel (dummy) nodes: simplify edge cases by ensuring head.next and tail.prev always exist
In-place reversal: three-pointer technique (prev, current, next_temp) — O(n) time, O(1) space
Cycle detection: Floyd's algorithm uses slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers — O(n) time, O(1) space
Finding the middle: slow and fast pointer — when fast reaches the end, slow is at the middle
Merging two sorted lists: compare heads, advance the smaller — O(n+m) time, O(1) space
The runner technique: two pointers moving at different speeds to find positions without counting
Memory allocation: each node is a separate heap allocation, contributing to memory fragmentation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a doubly linked list with sentinel nodes. Then solve three problems: (1) reverse a singly linked list in place, (2) detect a cycle and find its start using Floyd's algorithm, and (3) merge two sorted linked lists into one. Draw pointer diagrams for each step of the reversal.
setup_requirements: Understanding of basic linked lists from CS102
Whiteboard or diagram tool for pointer visualization

review.key: How does Floyd's cycle detection work?
review.value: Two pointers start at the head: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If there is a cycle, fast eventually catches slow inside the cycle. If fast reaches None, there is no cycle. To find the cycle start, reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time — they meet at the cycle entry.

review.key: Why use sentinel nodes?
review.value: Sentinel nodes eliminate special cases for empty lists and head/tail operations. Every insertion and deletion follows the same logic because there is always a valid previous and next node. This reduces bugs in code that handles edge cases.

review.key: What is the three-pointer reversal technique?
review.value: Maintain prev, current, and next_temp. In each iteration: save current.next in next_temp, point current.next to prev, advance prev to current, advance current to next_temp. When current is None, prev is the new head.

review.key: When is a circular linked list useful?
review.value: Round-robin scheduling (each process gets a turn, then wraps to the first), multiplayer game turns, circular buffers, and the Josephus problem. The key property is that traversal naturally wraps without special end-of-list logic.

learning_objectives: Implement stacks and queues using both arrays and linked lists
Solve advanced problems: expression evaluation, min-stack, queue from stacks
Understand the deque (double-ended queue) and its applications
Analyze the time complexity of all stack and queue operations
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 1.3 - Bags, Queues, and Stacks
Cracking the Coding Interview, Ch. 3 - Stacks and Queues

lecture_notes.introduction:
You implemented basic stacks and queues in CS102. Now we study them as abstract data types with multiple implementation strategies and explore their advanced applications. The choice between array-based and linked implementations involves trade-offs in memory usage, cache performance, and worst-case guarantees.

An array-based stack is simple: push increments a top pointer and stores the element; pop decrements and returns. The capacity is fixed unless you use a dynamic array. A linked-list stack pushes and pops at the head, giving O(1) operations without capacity limits but with higher memory overhead per element.

Advanced stack applications include expression evaluation (converting infix to postfix and evaluating postfix), the min-stack problem (maintaining O(1) minimum alongside O(1) push/pop), and using two stacks to simulate a queue. These problems appear frequently in interviews and demonstrate how simple data structures compose into powerful solutions.

The deque (double-ended queue) supports O(1) insertion and deletion at both ends. It generalizes both stacks and queues: a deque used at one end is a stack; used at opposite ends it is a queue. Python's collections.deque and Java's ArrayDeque are the standard implementations, built on circular arrays.
key_concepts: Array-based stack: push/pop at one end of an array — O(1), capacity limited (or use dynamic array for amortized O(1))
Linked-list stack: push/pop at head — O(1) always, no capacity limit, higher memory per element
Array-based queue (circular): enqueue at tail, dequeue at head, wrap around — O(1), fixed capacity
Linked-list queue: enqueue at tail, dequeue at head — O(1) with tail pointer, no capacity limit
Infix to postfix conversion: use a stack to hold operators, output operands immediately, respect precedence
Postfix evaluation: push operands, pop two and apply when an operator is encountered
Min-stack: maintain a parallel stack tracking the current minimum — O(1) getMin, O(1) push/pop
Queue from two stacks: push to inStack, pop from outStack (lazy transfer) — O(1) amortized dequeue
Deque (double-ended queue): O(1) insert/remove at both ends, generalizes stack and queue
Circular array implementation: use modular arithmetic (index % capacity) to wrap around
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a postfix expression evaluator using a stack. Then implement a MinStack that supports push, pop, and getMin all in O(1). Finally, implement a queue using two stacks and prove the amortized O(1) dequeue bound using the accounting method.
setup_requirements: Understanding of stacks and queues from CS102
Familiarity with amortized analysis from Week 2

review.key: How does a circular array queue work?
review.value: Maintain head and tail indices that wrap around using modulo: enqueue at tail % capacity, dequeue at head % capacity. The queue is full when (tail + 1) % capacity == head. This avoids shifting elements on dequeue.

review.key: How does the MinStack maintain O(1) getMin?
review.value: Maintain a second stack that records the minimum at each level. On push, push min(value, current_min) onto the min stack. On pop, pop from both stacks. getMin returns the top of the min stack.

review.key: How does a queue from two stacks achieve amortized O(1)?
review.value: Push to inStack. When outStack is empty and dequeue is called, transfer all elements from inStack to outStack (reversing order). Each element is moved at most twice (once to inStack, once to outStack), so n operations cost O(n) total.

review.key: When would you use a deque?
review.value: When you need efficient insertion and removal at both ends: sliding window problems, BFS with priority modifications, palindrome checking, and implementing both stack and queue with a single data structure.

learning_objectives: Understand tree terminology, properties, and traversal methods
Implement a binary search tree with insert, search, delete, and traversal operations
Analyze BST operations in terms of tree height
Perform in-order, pre-order, post-order, and level-order traversals
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 3.2 - Binary Search Trees
Cracking the Coding Interview, Ch. 4 - Trees and Graphs

lecture_notes.introduction:
Trees are hierarchical data structures that appear everywhere in computing: file systems, HTML DOM, database indexes, compiler parse trees, decision trees in machine learning, and organizational charts. Understanding trees is non-negotiable for any software engineer.

A binary search tree (BST) maintains the invariant that for every node, all keys in the left subtree are smaller and all keys in the right subtree are larger. This invariant enables O(h) search, insert, and delete, where h is the height of the tree. For a balanced tree, h = O(log n), giving the same performance as binary search on a sorted array but with O(log n) insertion and deletion — a major improvement over the O(n) cost of inserting into a sorted array.

The catch is that BSTs can become unbalanced. If elements are inserted in sorted order, the tree degenerates into a linked list with h = n, making all operations O(n). This motivates balanced BSTs (AVL trees, red-black trees) that maintain O(log n) height through rotations, which we study next week.

Tree traversals are fundamental operations. In-order traversal visits nodes in sorted order and is the basis for range queries. Pre-order traversal visits the root first and is used for serialization. Post-order visits children before the root and is used for expression evaluation and memory deallocation. Level-order (BFS) visits by depth and is used for finding shortest paths in unweighted trees.
key_concepts: Tree terminology: root, parent, child, leaf, internal node, height, depth, level, subtree
Binary tree: each node has at most two children (left and right)
BST invariant: for every node n, all keys in left subtree < n.key < all keys in right subtree
BST search: compare key with current node, go left if smaller, right if larger — O(h)
BST insert: search for the key's position, create a new leaf node there — O(h)
BST delete (three cases): leaf (remove), one child (replace with child), two children (replace with in-order successor)
In-order traversal (left, root, right): visits BST nodes in sorted order — O(n)
Pre-order traversal (root, left, right): useful for copying/serializing the tree — O(n)
Post-order traversal (left, right, root): useful for deleting the tree or evaluating expressions — O(n)
Level-order traversal (BFS with queue): visits nodes by depth level — O(n)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a BST by inserting the sequence [15, 10, 20, 5, 12, 25, 14]. Perform all four traversal types and verify in-order produces sorted output. Walk through all three delete cases: delete 25 (leaf), delete 20 (one child), delete 10 (two children, replaced by in-order successor 12). Draw the tree at each step.

review.key: What is the BST invariant?
review.value: For every node, all keys in the left subtree are less than the node's key, and all keys in the right subtree are greater. This invariant enables O(h) search by eliminating half the remaining tree at each comparison.

review.key: Why do BST operations depend on height, not size?
review.value: Each operation follows a single root-to-leaf path, visiting one node per level. The number of levels is the height h. For a balanced tree h = O(log n); for a degenerate tree h = O(n).

review.key: How does BST deletion handle two children?
review.value: Replace the deleted node with its in-order successor (smallest node in the right subtree) or in-order predecessor (largest in left subtree). Copy the successor's key, then recursively delete the successor (which has at most one child).

review.key: What causes a BST to degenerate?
review.value: Inserting elements in sorted (or reverse-sorted) order creates a tree where every node has only one child, forming a linked list. The height becomes n instead of log n, and all operations become O(n).

class_schedule.topic: Balanced Trees: AVL Trees and Red-Black Trees
learning_objectives: Understand why balancing is necessary and what balance guarantees O(log n) height
Implement AVL tree rotations: single and double rotations
Understand the red-black tree invariants and how they maintain balance
Compare AVL and red-black trees in terms of balance strictness and rotation frequency
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 3.3 - Balanced Search Trees

lecture_notes.introduction:
Unbalanced BSTs degenerate to O(n) operations when elements arrive in sorted order. Balanced BSTs solve this by maintaining invariants that guarantee O(log n) height through structural adjustments called rotations. AVL trees and red-black trees are the two most important balanced BST variants.

An AVL tree maintains the invariant that for every node, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most 1. When an insertion or deletion violates this invariant, the tree performs one or two rotations to restore balance. The four rotation cases (left-left, right-right, left-right, right-left) handle all imbalance scenarios.

A rotation restructures a subtree by changing which node is the root while preserving the BST invariant. A single rotation handles the case where the imbalance and the inserted node are on the same side (left-left or right-right). A double rotation (rotate the child, then rotate the node) handles the case where they are on opposite sides (left-right or right-left).

Red-black trees use a different strategy: each node is colored red or black, and five invariants (root is black, leaves (NIL) are black, red nodes have black children, every path has the same black count, etc.) guarantee that the longest path is at most twice the shortest. This weaker balance guarantee means fewer rotations per operation than AVL trees, making red-black trees preferred for high-throughput applications. Java's TreeMap and Linux's process scheduler use red-black trees.
key_concepts: AVL invariant: for every node, |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1
Balance factor: height(left) - height(right); values outside [-1, 1] trigger rebalancing
Single rotation (left or right): fixes same-side imbalance (LL or RR case) — O(1)
Double rotation (left-right or right-left): fixes opposite-side imbalance — two single rotations — O(1)
AVL insertion: insert as in BST, then walk up updating heights and rotating as needed — O(log n)
Red-black tree invariants: root is black, NIL leaves are black, red has black children, equal black height on all paths
Red-black trees guarantee height <= 2 * log2(n+1), which is O(log n)
Red-black insertion: insert red node, then fix violations with recoloring and at most 2 rotations
AVL vs red-black: AVL is more strictly balanced (faster lookups) but requires more rotations (slower inserts/deletes)
Self-balancing trees guarantee O(log n) for insert, delete, and search in all cases
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement AVL tree insertion with all four rotation cases. Insert elements in sorted order [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] and show how rotations maintain balance at each step, producing a tree of height 2 instead of a degenerate chain of height 6. Visualize the tree structure before and after each rotation.
setup_requirements: BST implementation from previous week as a starting point

review.key: Why is AVL balancing necessary?
review.value: Without balancing, sorted insertions create a linked-list tree with O(n) operations. AVL maintains |left_height - right_height| <= 1, guaranteeing height O(log n) and therefore O(log n) operations.

review.key: When do you use a single vs double rotation?
review.value: Single rotation (LL or RR): the imbalance and the causing node are on the same side. Double rotation (LR or RL): they are on opposite sides. The double rotation first rotates the child to create a same-side case, then rotates the node.

review.key: Why are red-black trees used more than AVL in practice?
review.value: Red-black trees require at most 2 rotations per insertion (vs up to O(log n) for AVL). Their weaker balance means slightly deeper trees but significantly fewer restructuring operations, making them faster for insert-heavy workloads.

review.key: What does the red-black black-height invariant guarantee?
review.value: Every root-to-leaf path has the same number of black nodes. Combined with 'no two consecutive reds', this ensures the longest path is at most twice the shortest, guaranteeing O(log n) height.

learning_objectives: Understand the heap property and the binary heap data structure
Implement insert and extract-min/max operations with heapify
Use a heap to implement a priority queue ADT
Implement heapsort and analyze its O(n log n) complexity

lecture_notes.introduction:
A priority queue is an abstract data type where each element has a priority, and elements are removed in priority order rather than insertion order. Priority queues are essential for scheduling (processes, network packets, hospital patients), graph algorithms (Dijkstra's shortest path, Prim's minimum spanning tree), and event-driven simulations.

The binary heap is the standard implementation of a priority queue. It is a complete binary tree (all levels full except possibly the last, which is filled left to right) satisfying the heap property: every node's priority is higher than (or equal to) its children's. A min-heap has the smallest element at the root; a max-heap has the largest.

The elegant insight of binary heaps is that a complete binary tree can be stored in an array without pointers. The root is at index 0, and for a node at index i, its left child is at 2i+1, its right child at 2i+2, and its parent at (i-1)/2. This array representation is more cache-friendly and memory-efficient than a pointer-based tree.

Insertion adds the element at the end and 'sifts up' (swaps with parent while the heap property is violated). Extraction removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and 'sifts down' (swaps with the smaller child while violated). Both operations are O(log n) because the tree height is exactly floor(log2 n). Building a heap from n elements takes O(n) using the bottom-up heapify algorithm, not O(n log n) — a surprising result we will prove.
key_concepts: Heap property (min-heap): every node's key is <= its children's keys; root holds the minimum
Complete binary tree: all levels full except the last, which is filled left to right
Array representation: root at 0, left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, parent at (i-1)//2
Insert (sift up): add at end, swap with parent while parent is larger — O(log n)
Extract-min (sift down): replace root with last element, swap with smaller child while violated — O(log n)
Build-heap (bottom-up): heapify each node from the bottom — O(n) total, not O(n log n)
Priority queue ADT: insert(key, priority), extract_min() or extract_max(), peek()
Heapsort: build a max-heap, repeatedly extract max to the end — O(n log n), in-place
Python's heapq module: heappush, heappop, heapify — min-heap only, negate for max-heap
Decrease-key operation: needed for Dijkstra's algorithm — O(log n) with index tracking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a MinHeap class with insert, extract_min, and build_heap methods. Demonstrate the build process visually on the array [9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 1, 8, 7, 4], showing how bottom-up heapify transforms it into a valid min-heap. Then implement heapsort and compare its performance with merge sort on large random arrays.
setup_requirements: Understanding of complete binary trees and array indexing

review.key: Why is building a heap O(n) and not O(n log n)?
review.value: Most nodes are near the bottom and need little sifting. Half the nodes are leaves (0 swaps), a quarter need 1 swap, an eighth need 2, etc. The total is n * sum(i/2^i) for i=0 to log n, which converges to O(n).

review.key: Why store a heap in an array instead of a tree with pointers?
review.value: Array storage is more memory-efficient (no pointer overhead), more cache-friendly (contiguous memory), and the parent/child relationships are computed with simple arithmetic instead of pointer dereferences.

review.key: What is the difference between a heap and a BST?
review.value: A BST orders elements left-to-right (left < root < right). A heap orders top-to-bottom (parent < children in min-heap). BST supports O(log n) search; heap does not support efficient search but provides O(1) peek at the min/max.

review.key: Is heapsort better than merge sort?
review.value: Heapsort is in-place (O(1) extra space vs O(n) for merge sort) but has worse cache performance and is not stable. In practice, merge sort and Timsort are faster due to cache locality and adaptive behavior on partially sorted data.

learning_objectives: Design hash functions with good distribution properties
Implement hash tables with separate chaining and open addressing
Analyze average and worst-case performance under different load factors
Understand the relationship between hash tables and Python dicts / Java HashMaps

lecture_notes.introduction:
Hash tables provide the fastest practical data structure for key-value lookup: O(1) average case for insert, delete, and search. They power Python dictionaries, Java HashMaps, database indexes, caches, symbol tables in compilers, and routing tables in network switches. Understanding hash tables deeply is essential for systems design.

A hash function maps keys to integer indices in an array of 'buckets.' The ideal hash function distributes keys uniformly across buckets so that collisions (two keys mapping to the same bucket) are rare. In practice, collisions are inevitable — the pigeonhole principle guarantees collisions when the key space exceeds the number of buckets.

Two collision resolution strategies dominate. Separate chaining stores colliding elements in a linked list at each bucket. Open addressing probes alternative buckets within the array itself. Chaining is simpler and degrades gracefully; open addressing has better cache performance but suffers from clustering at high load factors.

The load factor (n/m, elements divided by buckets) determines average chain length in chaining and collision probability in open addressing. When the load factor exceeds a threshold (typically 0.75), the table doubles its bucket count and rehashes all elements. This resize is O(n) but happens so rarely that the amortized cost of insert remains O(1).
key_concepts: Hash function requirements: deterministic, uniform distribution, fast computation
Division method: h(k) = k mod m; choose m as a prime not close to a power of 2
Multiplication method: h(k) = floor(m * (k * A mod 1)) where A is an irrational constant (often golden ratio)
Separate chaining: each bucket holds a linked list of colliding elements
Open addressing — linear probing: h(k, i) = (h(k) + i) mod m; causes primary clustering
Open addressing — quadratic probing: h(k, i) = (h(k) + c1*i + c2*i^2) mod m; reduces clustering
Open addressing — double hashing: h(k, i) = (h1(k) + i*h2(k)) mod m; eliminates clustering
Load factor alpha = n/m; average chain length in chaining is alpha; O(1 + alpha) expected lookup
Dynamic resizing: double the table and rehash all elements when alpha exceeds threshold
Universal hashing: choose hash function randomly from a family to guarantee O(1) expected time against any input
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a hash table from scratch with separate chaining. Insert 10,000 random keys and visualize the distribution of chain lengths to demonstrate uniform hashing. Then implement open addressing with linear probing and show how primary clustering develops as the load factor approaches 1.0.
setup_requirements: Understanding of linked lists and arrays
Random number generator for key distribution testing

review.key: What is the load factor and why does it matter?
review.value: Load factor alpha = n/m (elements / buckets). In chaining, the average chain length is alpha, so lookup is O(1 + alpha). When alpha exceeds ~0.75, resize and rehash to keep chains short.

review.key: What is primary clustering in linear probing?
review.value: Occupied slots form contiguous runs. A collision near a cluster forces probing through the entire cluster, making the cluster longer and future collisions more likely. This positive feedback loop degrades performance as load factor increases.

review.key: Why must hash table keys be immutable?
review.value: The hash value determines the bucket. If a key mutates after insertion, its hash changes, but it remains in the original bucket. Looking it up with the new hash searches the wrong bucket, making the element unfindable.

review.key: What is the worst case for a hash table?
review.value: O(n) when all keys hash to the same bucket, creating a single chain of length n. Universal hashing prevents adversarial inputs from causing this, and good hash functions make it astronomically unlikely for random inputs.

learning_objectives: Represent graphs using adjacency lists, adjacency matrices, and edge lists
Classify graphs: directed vs undirected, weighted vs unweighted, cyclic vs acyclic
Analyze the space complexity of different graph representations
Model real-world problems as graph problems

lecture_notes.introduction:
Graphs are the most versatile data structure in computer science. They model relationships between entities: social networks (people connected by friendships), road maps (intersections connected by roads), dependency chains (tasks connected by prerequisites), web pages (pages connected by links), and molecular structures (atoms connected by bonds).

A graph G = (V, E) consists of vertices (nodes) V and edges (connections) E. The fundamental design decision is how to represent the edges. An adjacency list stores, for each vertex, a list of its neighbors. An adjacency matrix stores a |V| x |V| grid where entry (i, j) is 1 (or the edge weight) if there is an edge from i to j. An edge list simply stores all edges as (u, v) pairs.

The choice of representation affects space usage and operation speed. Adjacency lists use O(V + E) space and are efficient for sparse graphs (few edges relative to vertices). Adjacency matrices use O(V^2) space but allow O(1) edge existence queries. Most real-world graphs (social networks, road maps, web graphs) are sparse, making adjacency lists the standard choice.

Graph theory provides a precise vocabulary for reasoning about structure: paths, cycles, connectivity, bipartiteness, planarity, and more. This session establishes the terminology and representation choices that underlie all graph algorithms.
key_concepts: Graph G = (V, E): V is the set of vertices, E is the set of edges connecting them
Directed graph (digraph): edges have direction (u -> v); undirected: edges are bidirectional
Weighted graph: each edge has a numeric weight (distance, cost, capacity)
Adjacency list: array of lists — vertex i stores a list of its neighbors — O(V + E) space
Adjacency matrix: 2D array — matrix[i][j] = 1 if edge (i,j) exists — O(V^2) space, O(1) edge query
Sparse graph: E << V^2 (most real graphs); dense graph: E close to V^2
Degree of a vertex: number of edges incident to it; in-degree and out-degree for directed graphs
Path: sequence of vertices connected by edges; cycle: path that starts and ends at the same vertex
Connected graph: there is a path between every pair of vertices (undirected); strongly connected (directed)
DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph): a directed graph with no cycles — models dependency ordering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a campus map as a graph with buildings as vertices and walkways as weighted edges. Implement the graph using both adjacency list (dictionary of lists) and adjacency matrix (2D array). Compare the space usage and operation times. Then model a course prerequisite system as a DAG and visualize the dependency structure.
setup_requirements: Graph visualization tool (networkx + matplotlib, or online tool)

review.key: When do you use adjacency lists vs adjacency matrices?
review.value: Adjacency lists for sparse graphs (E << V^2): uses O(V+E) space, efficient iteration over neighbors. Adjacency matrices for dense graphs or when O(1) edge queries are needed: uses O(V^2) space.

review.key: What is the difference between a directed and undirected graph?
review.value: In a directed graph, edge (u,v) goes from u to v but not necessarily v to u. In an undirected graph, every edge connects both directions. An undirected edge {u,v} is stored as both (u,v) and (v,u) in an adjacency list.

review.key: What is a DAG and why is it important?
review.value: A Directed Acyclic Graph has directed edges and no cycles. DAGs model dependencies (course prerequisites, build systems, task scheduling). Topological sort orders vertices so all edges go from earlier to later.

review.key: How do you represent a weighted graph?
review.value: In an adjacency list, store (neighbor, weight) pairs instead of just neighbors. In an adjacency matrix, store the weight instead of 1 (use infinity for non-edges). In an edge list, store (u, v, weight) triples.

learning_objectives: Implement breadth-first search and depth-first search on graphs
Apply BFS to find shortest paths in unweighted graphs
Apply DFS to detect cycles, perform topological sorting, and find connected components
Analyze the time and space complexity of graph traversals
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 4.1-4.2 - Graph traversal sections

lecture_notes.introduction:
BFS and DFS are the two fundamental graph traversal algorithms. Every graph algorithm you will ever encounter either is or builds upon one of these traversals. BFS explores level by level (all neighbors before going deeper), while DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Their different exploration patterns make them suited to different problems.

BFS uses a queue and visits vertices in order of their distance from the source. This makes BFS the correct algorithm for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs: the first time BFS reaches a vertex, it has found the shortest path. BFS also detects whether a graph is bipartite (two-colorable) by checking if adjacent vertices can always receive different colors.

DFS uses a stack (or recursion) and explores as deep as possible along each branch. DFS classifies edges into tree edges, back edges, forward edges, and cross edges. Back edges indicate cycles. The absence of back edges means the graph is acyclic. DFS on a DAG produces a topological ordering when vertices are output in reverse finish order.

Both traversals run in O(V + E) time because they visit every vertex and examine every edge exactly once (twice for undirected graphs). This linear-time guarantee makes them practical for graphs with millions of vertices and edges.
key_concepts: BFS: explore all vertices at distance d before distance d+1 — use a queue — O(V + E)
BFS shortest path: the first time BFS visits a vertex, it has found the shortest path (in unweighted graphs)
BFS tracks distance and parent pointers to reconstruct shortest paths
DFS: explore as deep as possible, then backtrack — use recursion or explicit stack — O(V + E)
DFS discovery and finish times: used for edge classification and topological sort
Edge classification: tree edge (part of DFS tree), back edge (ancestor, indicates cycle), forward edge, cross edge
Cycle detection: a directed graph has a cycle if and only if DFS finds a back edge
Topological sort: order vertices of a DAG so all edges go from earlier to later — reverse DFS finish order
Connected components (undirected): run DFS/BFS from each unvisited vertex — each run finds one component
Strongly connected components (directed): Kosaraju's or Tarjan's algorithm using two DFS passes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement BFS on a social network graph to find the shortest connection path between two people. Implement DFS on a course prerequisite graph to produce a topological ordering (a valid course sequence). Show cycle detection by adding a circular dependency and demonstrating the back edge detection.
setup_requirements: Sample graphs for social network and course prerequisites

review.key: Why does BFS find shortest paths?
review.value: BFS explores vertices in order of increasing distance from the source. The first time it reaches vertex v, it has traversed the minimum number of edges. This fails for weighted graphs where edge weights vary.

review.key: How does DFS detect cycles?
review.value: In a directed graph, DFS finds a cycle if and only if it discovers a back edge — an edge from a vertex to one of its ancestors in the DFS tree. The ancestor is currently being processed (on the recursion stack).

review.key: What is topological sort and when is it used?
review.value: A linear ordering of DAG vertices such that for every edge (u,v), u appears before v. Used for: build order, course scheduling, task dependency resolution. Computed by DFS in reverse finish order.

review.key: What is the time complexity of BFS and DFS?
review.value: Both are O(V + E): they visit every vertex once (O(V)) and examine every edge once (O(E)). This is optimal since you cannot know the graph structure without examining all vertices and edges.

learning_objectives: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm for single-source shortest paths with non-negative weights
Understand the Bellman-Ford algorithm for graphs with negative weights
Use priority queues to achieve optimal Dijkstra performance
Apply shortest path algorithms to real-world routing problems

lecture_notes.introduction:
Finding shortest paths in weighted graphs is one of the most practically important problems in computer science. GPS navigation finds the shortest route between locations. Network routing finds the fastest path for data packets. Social network analysis finds the closest connection between people. Logistics systems find the cheapest shipping routes.

Dijkstra's algorithm solves the single-source shortest path problem for graphs with non-negative edge weights. Starting from a source vertex, it greedily extends the shortest known path to the nearest unvisited vertex. The key insight is that once a vertex is 'settled' (its shortest distance is confirmed), it never needs to be reconsidered. This greedy property only holds when edge weights are non-negative.

The algorithm's efficiency depends on how the 'nearest unvisited vertex' is found. A naive scan takes O(V) per step, giving O(V^2) total. Using a binary heap (priority queue) reduces this to O((V + E) log V). For dense graphs V^2 ~ E, the naive scan is actually faster; for sparse graphs, the heap version is better.

Bellman-Ford handles graphs with negative edge weights (but no negative cycles). It relaxes all edges V-1 times, guaranteeing shortest paths because the longest possible shortest path has V-1 edges. It is slower than Dijkstra (O(VE) vs O((V+E) log V)) but more general.
key_concepts: Shortest path problem: find the minimum-weight path from source s to all other vertices
Relaxation: if dist[u] + weight(u,v) < dist[v], update dist[v] — the core operation of all shortest path algorithms
Dijkstra's algorithm: greedily settle the nearest vertex, relax its neighbors — O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap
Dijkstra requires non-negative edge weights; negative weights break the greedy property
Priority queue in Dijkstra: extract-min gives the next vertex to settle; decrease-key updates distances
Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times — handles negative weights — O(VE)
Negative cycle detection: if any edge can still be relaxed after V-1 iterations, a negative cycle exists
Shortest path tree: the parent pointers form a tree rooted at the source, encoding all shortest paths
Dijkstra on a grid: models pathfinding in games and robotics (4-connected or 8-connected)
A* search: Dijkstra with a heuristic that estimates remaining distance — faster in practice for point-to-point
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm with a priority queue on a road network graph (cities as vertices, roads as weighted edges). Find the shortest path from a source city to all others, outputting the path and distance. Then demonstrate that Dijkstra fails on a graph with a negative edge and show Bellman-Ford producing the correct result.

review.key: Why does Dijkstra fail with negative edge weights?
review.value: Dijkstra's greedy property assumes that once a vertex is settled, no shorter path exists. A negative edge from an unsettled vertex could provide a shorter path to an already-settled vertex, violating this assumption.

review.key: What is relaxation?
review.value: Relaxation checks if a path through vertex u provides a shorter route to v: if dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v], update dist[v] and set parent[v] = u. Every shortest path algorithm is built on relaxation.

review.key: Why does Bellman-Ford run V-1 iterations?
review.value: The longest shortest path in a graph with V vertices has at most V-1 edges. Each iteration finds shortest paths using one more edge. After V-1 iterations, all shortest paths (of any length) have been found.

review.key: When would you use Bellman-Ford over Dijkstra?
review.value: When the graph has negative edge weights (e.g., financial arbitrage, network cost optimization) or when you need to detect negative cycles. For non-negative weights, Dijkstra is always faster.

learning_objectives: Implement and analyze quicksort with various pivot strategies
Prove the O(n log n) lower bound for comparison-based sorting
Understand non-comparison sorts: counting sort, radix sort, bucket sort
Choose the optimal sorting algorithm based on data characteristics
readings: CLRS, Ch. 8 - Sorting in Linear Time

lecture_notes.introduction:
Sorting is the most well-studied problem in computer science. This session provides a comprehensive analysis of sorting algorithms, from the practical quicksort to the theoretical lower bound proving that O(n log n) is optimal for comparison sorts, to the linear-time sorts that beat this bound by using element structure.

Quicksort is the most widely used sorting algorithm in practice. It partitions the array around a pivot element: elements less than the pivot go left, greater go right. Then it recursively sorts each partition. With a good pivot (near the median), each partition halves the array, giving O(n log n) performance. With a bad pivot (smallest or largest element), one partition has n-1 elements, giving O(n^2). Randomized pivot selection makes the worst case astronomically unlikely.

The lower bound proof uses a decision tree model: any comparison sort corresponds to a binary tree where each leaf is a permutation of the input. With n! possible permutations, the tree needs at least n! leaves, requiring height at least log2(n!) = Omega(n log n). No comparison sort can beat this.

Non-comparison sorts sidestep this bound by using the structure of keys. Counting sort distributes elements into buckets indexed by key value — O(n + k) where k is the key range. Radix sort applies counting sort digit by digit — O(d(n + b)) for d digits in base b. These achieve linear time for keys with bounded range, but they do not work for arbitrary comparison-based ordering.
key_concepts: Quicksort: partition around pivot, recursively sort partitions — O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst case
Partition: rearrange array so elements < pivot are left, elements > pivot are right — O(n)
Pivot strategies: first element (bad for sorted input), random (good in practice), median-of-three (best practical)
Quicksort is in-place (O(log n) stack space) and has small constant factors, making it fast in practice
Lower bound: any comparison sort requires Omega(n log n) comparisons in the worst case
Decision tree model: n! leaves (permutations), binary tree height >= log2(n!) = Omega(n log n)
Counting sort: count occurrences, compute positions, place elements — O(n + k), stable, not in-place
Radix sort: sort by each digit using a stable sort (counting sort) — O(d(n + b)), works for fixed-width keys
Bucket sort: distribute elements into buckets, sort each bucket — O(n) expected for uniformly distributed input
Stability: merge sort and counting sort are stable; quicksort and heapsort are not
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement quicksort with three pivot strategies (first, random, median-of-three) and benchmark on random, sorted, and reverse-sorted arrays. Demonstrate the O(n^2) worst case with first-element pivot on sorted input. Then implement counting sort and show it sorting 1 million integers in the range [0, 1000] faster than quicksort.
setup_requirements: Understanding of recursion and partition logic

review.key: Why is quicksort fast in practice despite O(n^2) worst case?
review.value: Randomized pivot selection makes worst case astronomically unlikely. Quicksort's inner loop is simple (just a comparison and swap), its cache performance is excellent (in-place, sequential access), and its constant factor is smaller than merge sort's.

review.key: What does the O(n log n) lower bound prove?
review.value: No comparison-based sorting algorithm can guarantee fewer than Omega(n log n) comparisons in the worst case. This means merge sort and heapsort are asymptotically optimal among comparison sorts.

review.key: When is counting sort better than quicksort?
review.value: When the key range k is O(n) or smaller. Counting sort takes O(n + k) time, which is O(n) when k = O(n). For large key ranges (k >> n), the extra memory and initialization make counting sort impractical.

review.key: What is a stable sort and when does it matter?
review.value: A stable sort preserves the relative order of equal elements. It matters when sorting by multiple keys: sort by secondary key first (stable), then by primary key (stable). If the primary sort is unstable, it can scramble the secondary ordering.

learning_objectives: Identify problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Solve problems using both top-down (memoized) and bottom-up (tabulated) approaches
Implement classic DP problems: Fibonacci, coin change, longest common subsequence, knapsack
Analyze the time and space complexity of dynamic programming solutions
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 6.6 - Dynamic Programming (if available)

lecture_notes.introduction:
Dynamic programming (DP) is an algorithm design technique for optimization problems where the solution can be constructed from solutions to overlapping subproblems. It is one of the most powerful and widely tested topics in algorithm design, appearing in technical interviews, competitive programming, and real-world optimization.

The key insight is that some problems have optimal substructure: the optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems. The Fibonacci sequence has this property: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). But naive recursion recomputes the same subproblems exponentially many times. DP eliminates this redundancy by storing results in a table.

Two approaches exist. Top-down (memoization) writes the recursive solution and adds a cache — the structure of the recursion drives the computation. Bottom-up (tabulation) fills a table iteratively from the smallest subproblems upward — the loop structure drives the computation. Both produce the same result; the choice is a matter of style and sometimes performance.

The hardest part of DP is formulating the recurrence: defining what a subproblem is, what the recursive relationship is, and what the base cases are. Once you have the recurrence, the coding is mechanical. This session studies the technique through four classic problems: Fibonacci (warmup), coin change (counting and optimization variants), longest common subsequence (string comparison), and 0/1 knapsack (resource allocation).
key_concepts: Optimal substructure: the optimal solution to a problem contains optimal solutions to its subproblems
Overlapping subproblems: the same subproblems are solved multiple times in a naive recursive approach
Top-down (memoization): recursive solution with a cache storing previously computed results
Bottom-up (tabulation): iterative solution filling a table from base cases upward
Coin change: minimum coins to make amount n — recurrence: dp[n] = 1 + min(dp[n-c] for c in coins)
Longest Common Subsequence (LCS): dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if match, else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
0/1 Knapsack: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-wi] + vi) — include or exclude item i
State definition: the subproblem parameters define the table dimensions (1D, 2D, etc.)
Space optimization: many 2D DP problems only need the previous row, reducing O(nm) space to O(m)
Reconstructing the solution: trace back through the table to find which choices led to the optimal value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve four problems step by step: (1) Fibonacci with memoization and tabulation, (2) coin change minimum coins using bottom-up DP, (3) LCS of two strings with a 2D table and backtracking to reconstruct the subsequence, (4) 0/1 knapsack with item selection reconstruction. For each, draw the DP table and show the recurrence filling it.
setup_requirements: Understanding of recursion and memoization from CS102
Whiteboard or table for DP visualization

review.key: How do you identify a DP problem?
review.value: Look for two properties: (1) optimal substructure — can you build the solution from smaller solutions? (2) overlapping subproblems — does a naive recursive solution recompute the same subproblems? If both hold, DP applies.

review.key: What is the difference between memoization and tabulation?
review.value: Memoization is top-down: start with the original problem, recurse, and cache results. Tabulation is bottom-up: start with the smallest subproblems and iteratively build up. Tabulation avoids recursion overhead; memoization only computes needed subproblems.

review.key: How do you define the state for a DP problem?
review.value: The state is the minimum set of parameters that uniquely identifies a subproblem. For coin change, it is the remaining amount. For LCS, it is the two string positions (i, j). For knapsack, it is the item index and remaining capacity (i, w).

review.key: How do you optimize DP space complexity?
review.value: If the recurrence only depends on the previous row (or previous few entries), you can use a rolling array instead of the full table. The 2D knapsack O(nW) table reduces to O(W) by keeping only two rows.

learning_objectives: Understand the greedy strategy and when it produces optimal solutions
Prove greedy correctness using exchange arguments and greedy-stays-ahead arguments
Implement classic greedy algorithms: activity selection, Huffman coding, Kruskal's MST
Distinguish problems where greedy works from those requiring DP
readings: Sedgewick, Algorithms, Ch. 4.3 - Minimum Spanning Trees

lecture_notes.introduction:
Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find a globally optimal solution. Unlike dynamic programming, which considers all possibilities, a greedy algorithm commits to the best immediate choice without reconsidering. This makes greedy algorithms simpler and faster, but they only work when the problem has the greedy choice property.

The greedy choice property means that making the locally optimal choice does not prevent finding the globally optimal solution. For the activity selection problem (schedule the maximum number of non-overlapping activities), the greedy choice is to always select the activity that finishes earliest. This leaves maximum room for future activities.

Proving greedy correctness requires showing that the greedy choice is always part of some optimal solution. The exchange argument shows that any optimal solution can be transformed to include the greedy choice without worsening it. The greedy-stays-ahead argument shows that the greedy solution is at least as good as any other solution at every step.

Not all optimization problems yield to greedy strategies. The 0/1 knapsack problem does not: taking the item with the best value-to-weight ratio can leave no room for a combination that would have been better. Distinguishing greedy-solvable problems from those requiring DP is a critical skill.
key_concepts: Greedy strategy: at each step, make the choice that looks best right now without reconsidering
Greedy choice property: a locally optimal choice is consistent with a globally optimal solution
Optimal substructure: after the greedy choice, the remaining problem is a smaller instance of the same problem
Activity selection: sort by finish time, greedily select earliest-finishing non-overlapping activity — O(n log n)
Huffman coding: build an optimal prefix-free code by repeatedly merging the two lowest-frequency symbols — O(n log n)
Minimum Spanning Tree: connect all vertices with minimum total edge weight
Kruskal's algorithm: sort edges by weight, add cheapest edge that does not create a cycle — O(E log E) with Union-Find
Prim's algorithm: grow the MST from a starting vertex, always adding the cheapest edge to a new vertex — O((V+E) log V)
Exchange argument: prove that swapping a non-greedy choice for the greedy choice does not worsen the solution
Greedy vs DP: fractional knapsack is greedy (take fractions); 0/1 knapsack requires DP (all-or-nothing)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve the activity selection problem step by step: sort by finish time, greedily select compatible activities. Then implement Kruskal's MST algorithm on a weighted graph using Union-Find for cycle detection. Show the exchange argument proof for activity selection on a concrete example where swapping one activity for the earliest-finishing one does not reduce the count.
setup_requirements: Weighted graph implementation from previous weeks

review.key: How do you prove a greedy algorithm is correct?
review.value: Exchange argument: show that any optimal solution can be modified to include the greedy choice without getting worse. This proves the greedy choice is safe. Combined with optimal substructure, it proves the greedy algorithm finds an optimal solution.

review.key: Why does greedy fail for 0/1 knapsack?
review.value: Taking the item with the best value/weight ratio might consume capacity that would have been better used by a different combination. Greedy cannot undo choices, so it misses the optimal combination. DP considers all combinations.

review.key: What is Huffman coding used for?
review.value: Data compression. Frequent characters get short codes; rare characters get long codes. The greedy algorithm builds the optimal code by merging the two least-frequent symbols at each step, creating a binary tree where code length = depth.

review.key: What is the difference between Kruskal's and Prim's MST algorithms?
review.value: Kruskal's processes edges globally (sort all edges, add cheapest non-cycle-forming edge). Prim's grows locally (from a vertex, always extend to nearest neighbor). Both produce the same MST. Kruskal's is better for sparse graphs; Prim's for dense.

learning_objectives: Synthesize all course topics into a unified understanding of data structures and algorithms
Compare data structures by their operation complexities and appropriate use cases
Review algorithm design paradigms: divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy
Prepare for the final examination with comprehensive problem solving
readings: Review all previous readings, focusing on complexity tables and algorithm summaries
CLRS, Appendix A - Summations (for series analysis review)
Course notes and lecture materials from all sessions

lecture_notes.introduction:
This final session synthesizes the entire course into a coherent framework for choosing data structures and designing algorithms. The goal is not to memorize facts but to develop the judgment to make informed decisions: given a problem, which data structure supports the needed operations efficiently? Given an optimization problem, which design paradigm applies?

Data structures form a spectrum of trade-offs. Arrays excel at random access but struggle with insertion. Linked lists excel at insertion but lack random access. Hash tables provide O(1) average lookup but have no ordering. BSTs provide O(log n) ordered operations. Heaps provide O(1) access to the minimum but nothing else efficiently. Graphs model arbitrary relationships. Each structure is optimal for specific access patterns.

Algorithm design paradigms provide frameworks for attacking new problems. Divide and conquer splits the problem, solves parts recursively, and combines. Dynamic programming builds optimal solutions from overlapping subproblems. Greedy algorithms make locally optimal choices. Recognizing which paradigm applies to a new problem is the most valuable skill you take from this course.

Looking ahead, this course's concepts appear in every area of computer science: databases use B-trees and hash indexes, operating systems use priority queues for scheduling, networks use shortest-path algorithms for routing, machine learning uses graph algorithms for clustering, and compilers use stacks and trees for parsing. You now have the vocabulary and analytical tools to engage with all of these systems.
key_concepts: Data structure comparison table: array, linked list, hash table, BST, heap, graph — operations and complexities
Choosing data structures: what operations do you need? What is the expected data size? Are ordering guarantees needed?
Algorithm paradigms: brute force, divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy — recognizing which applies
Divide and conquer: split into independent subproblems of the same type (merge sort, quicksort, binary search)
Dynamic programming: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure (knapsack, LCS, shortest paths)
Greedy: locally optimal choices lead to globally optimal solution (activity selection, Huffman, MST)
Complexity hierarchy: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
NP-completeness preview: some problems (TSP, graph coloring, SAT) have no known polynomial algorithm
Practical performance: constant factors, cache behavior, and memory allocation often matter more than asymptotic complexity for small inputs
Lifelong learning: the problems change, but the analytical framework and data structure toolkit you have built remain
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comprehensive problem-solving session: (1) Given a problem statement, choose the appropriate data structure and justify the choice. (2) Given a recursive solution, determine whether it has overlapping subproblems (use DP) or independent subproblems (divide and conquer). (3) Given an optimization problem, determine whether the greedy approach works or DP is needed. Work through 6-8 practice problems covering all course topics.
setup_requirements: All course materials available for reference
Practice problems from CLRS and interview prep resources

review.key: How do you choose a data structure for a new problem?
review.value: Identify the operations you need (insert, delete, search, min, ordered iteration) and their frequency. Match to the structure with the best complexity for those operations. Consider space constraints and whether ordering is needed.

review.key: How do you distinguish DP from greedy?
review.value: If the problem has overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure, try DP. If the greedy choice never needs to be reconsidered (proved by exchange argument), greedy is simpler and faster. When in doubt, DP is safer.

review.key: What is the most important skill from this course?
review.value: The ability to analyze a problem, choose appropriate data structures, design an efficient algorithm, and prove its correctness and complexity. This framework applies to every technical problem you will encounter.

review.key: Where do these concepts appear in practice?
review.value: Databases (B-trees, hash indexes), networks (shortest paths, spanning trees), operating systems (heaps for scheduling, queues for I/O), web search (graphs, hash tables), machine learning (trees, dynamic programming), and every technical interview.

quizzes.topic: Algorithm Analysis, Arrays, and Linked Lists

questions.answer: O(n log n). The outer while loop runs log2(n) times because i doubles each iteration (1, 2, 4, 8, ..., n). The inner for loop runs n times for each outer iteration. Total operations: n * log2(n) = O(n log n).

rubric.key: Correctly identifies outer loop as O(log n) due to doubling
Correctly identifies inner loop as O(n)
Correctly multiplies to O(n log n) with clear reasoning

questions.question: Explain the amortized O(1) bound for dynamic array append. Why does doubling the capacity (rather than adding a constant) give this result?
questions.answer: With doubling, after n appends the total resize work is 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + n = 2n - 1 (geometric series). Adding n regular appends gives total work 3n - 1, so amortized cost per append is O(1). With constant increment k, resizing happens every k appends at cost O(current_size). Total resize work over n appends is O(n^2/k) = O(n^2), making amortized append O(n).

rubric.key: Correctly explains the geometric series sum for doubling
Derives the O(1) amortized bound
Explains why constant increment gives O(n) amortized

questions.question:
Given the following BST, show the result of deleting node 15:

        15
       /  \
      10   20
     / \     \
    5   12   25
         \
         14

Explain which deletion case applies and why.
questions.answer:
Node 15 has two children, so we use the two-children case: replace 15 with its in-order successor. The in-order successor is the smallest node in the right subtree, which is 20. Copy 20's value to the root, then delete the original node 20 (which has one child, 25). Result:

        20
       /  \
      10   25
     / \
    5   12
         \
         14

rubric.key: Correctly identifies two-children deletion case
Correctly identifies in-order successor as 20
Correct final tree structure

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching for a value in an unbalanced BST vs a balanced BST? Give an example of an insertion sequence that produces the worst case.
questions.answer:
Unbalanced BST: O(n) worst case. Inserting elements in sorted order (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) creates a right-skewed chain where every node has only a right child. The height equals n, so search follows n nodes.

Balanced BST (AVL or Red-Black): O(log n) in all cases, because rotations maintain height at most O(log n) regardless of insertion order.

rubric.key: Correct O(n) for unbalanced with explanation
Provides concrete worst-case insertion sequence
Correct O(log n) for balanced with explanation

questions.question: Given the array [9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 1, 8, 7, 4], build a min-heap using bottom-up heapify. Show the array after each sift-down operation.
questions.answer:
Start from the last non-leaf node (index 3, value 2) and sift down each node:

Initial: [9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 1, 8, 7, 4]
Sift index 3 (2): children are 7 and 4. 2 < both, no change.
Sift index 2 (6): children are 1 and 8. Swap with 1: [9, 5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 8, 7, 4]
Sift index 1 (5): children are 2 and 3. Swap with 2: [9, 2, 1, 5, 3, 6, 8, 7, 4]. Then 5's children are 7 and 4. Swap with 4: [9, 2, 1, 4, 3, 6, 8, 7, 5]
Sift index 0 (9): children are 2 and 1. Swap with 1: [1, 2, 9, 4, 3, 6, 8, 7, 5]. Then 9's children are 6 and 8. Swap with 6: [1, 2, 6, 4, 3, 9, 8, 7, 5]

Final min-heap: [1, 2, 6, 4, 3, 9, 8, 7, 5]

rubric.key: Correct starting point (last non-leaf node)
Correct sift-down operations with swap decisions
Valid final min-heap (every parent <= children)

questions.question: Explain the difference between separate chaining and open addressing for collision resolution in hash tables. When would you prefer each?
questions.answer:
Separate chaining: each bucket holds a linked list of colliding elements. Advantages: simple implementation, graceful degradation at high load factors, easy deletion. Disadvantages: pointer overhead, poor cache locality, extra memory allocation.

Open addressing: collisions are resolved by probing alternative slots within the array. Advantages: better cache locality (all data in one array), no pointer overhead, less memory allocation. Disadvantages: clustering degrades performance at high load factors, deletion requires lazy deletion markers, load factor must stay below ~0.7.

Prefer chaining when: load factor may fluctuate, deletions are frequent, or simplicity matters. Prefer open addressing when: memory is tight, cache performance matters, or the load factor is well-controlled.

rubric.key: Accurately describes both strategies with pros/cons
Explains when each is preferred with reasoning

questions.question: What are the four rotation cases in an AVL tree? For each, describe the imbalance pattern and the rotation(s) needed to fix it.
questions.answer:
1. Left-Left (LL): Node's left child is left-heavy. Fix: single right rotation at the node.
2. Right-Right (RR): Node's right child is right-heavy. Fix: single left rotation at the node.
3. Left-Right (LR): Node's left child is right-heavy. Fix: left rotation at the child, then right rotation at the node.
4. Right-Left (RL): Node's right child is left-heavy. Fix: right rotation at the child, then left rotation at the node.

Single rotations fix same-side imbalances; double rotations fix opposite-side imbalances by first converting to a same-side case.

rubric.key: Correctly names all four cases with imbalance patterns
Correct rotation(s) for each case

questions.question:
Given this directed graph:
A -> B (weight 4), A -> C (weight 2), B -> D (weight 3), C -> B (weight 1), C -> D (weight 7), D -> E (weight 1), B -> E (weight 5)

Apply Dijkstra's algorithm from source A. Show the order in which vertices are settled and their final shortest distances.
questions.answer:
Initialize: dist = {A:0, B:inf, C:inf, D:inf, E:inf}

1. Settle A (dist 0). Relax A->B: dist[B]=4. Relax A->C: dist[C]=2.
2. Settle C (dist 2). Relax C->B: 2+1=3 < 4, dist[B]=3. Relax C->D: 2+7=9, dist[D]=9.
3. Settle B (dist 3). Relax B->D: 3+3=6 < 9, dist[D]=6. Relax B->E: 3+5=8, dist[E]=8.
4. Settle D (dist 6). Relax D->E: 6+1=7 < 8, dist[E]=7.
5. Settle E (dist 7).

Order: A(0), C(2), B(3), D(6), E(7).

rubric.key: Correct settlement order
Correct final distances for all vertices
Shows relaxation steps with comparisons

questions.question: Explain why BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs but not in weighted graphs. What algorithm should you use for weighted graphs?
questions.answer: BFS explores vertices in order of edge count (hops) from the source. In unweighted graphs, every edge has the same cost, so fewer hops always means shorter distance. In weighted graphs, a path with more hops can have lower total weight than a path with fewer hops (e.g., two edges of weight 1 beat one edge of weight 10). BFS would settle the single-hop path first, missing the cheaper two-hop path. Use Dijkstra's algorithm for weighted graphs with non-negative weights, or Bellman-Ford for graphs with negative weights.

rubric.key: Correctly explains why BFS works for unweighted graphs
Correctly explains the failure case for weighted graphs with a concrete example
Recommends appropriate algorithms for weighted graphs

questions.question: What is the time complexity of DFS and BFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges? Why is it O(V + E) and not O(V * E)?
questions.answer: Both are O(V + E). Each vertex is visited exactly once: O(V) total for vertex processing. Each edge is examined exactly once (in directed graphs) or twice (in undirected): O(E) total for edge examination. The work is additive, not multiplicative, because examining a vertex's edges is proportional to its degree (number of neighbors), and the sum of all degrees is 2E (undirected) or E (directed). O(V * E) would mean examining all E edges for each of V vertices, which does not happen — each vertex only examines its own adjacency list.

rubric.key: Correctly states O(V + E)
Explains V component (each vertex visited once)
Explains E component (each edge examined once) with degree sum argument
Explains why it is not O(V * E)

quizzes.topic: Sorting Analysis, Dynamic Programming, and Greedy Algorithms

questions.question: Explain why quicksort is generally faster than merge sort in practice despite having a worse worst-case complexity. Under what circumstances would you choose merge sort over quicksort?
questions.answer:
Quicksort is faster in practice because: (1) It sorts in-place with O(log n) stack space, while merge sort needs O(n) auxiliary space for merging. (2) Quicksort has better cache locality — it operates on contiguous memory without copying to a separate buffer. (3) Its inner loop is simple (one comparison, one swap), with smaller constant factors. (4) Randomized pivot selection makes the O(n^2) worst case astronomically unlikely.

Choose merge sort when: (1) Worst-case O(n log n) guarantee is required (safety-critical systems). (2) Stability is needed (preserving order of equal elements). (3) Sorting linked lists (no random access needed, and merge is naturally O(1) space for lists). (4) External sorting (data on disk doesn't fit in memory).

rubric.key: Explains cache locality and in-place advantages
Explains constant factor and inner loop simplicity
Gives valid scenarios where merge sort is preferred

questions.question: Solve the coin change problem: given coins [1, 5, 10, 25] and amount 36, find the minimum number of coins needed. Show the DP table for amounts 0 through 36.
questions.answer:
dp[0] = 0. For each amount a from 1 to 36: dp[a] = 1 + min(dp[a-c] for each coin c <= a).

Key values: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[5]=1, dp[6]=2, dp[10]=1, dp[11]=2, dp[15]=2, dp[20]=2, dp[25]=1, dp[26]=2, dp[30]=2, dp[35]=2, dp[36]=3.

Minimum coins for 36: dp[36] = 3 (using 25 + 10 + 1).

Recurrence: dp[36] = 1 + min(dp[35], dp[31], dp[26], dp[11]) = 1 + min(2, 4, 2, 2) = 3.

rubric.key: Correct recurrence relation
Correct final answer (3 coins)
Shows key DP table values with reasoning

questions.question: The activity selection problem asks: given n activities with start and finish times, select the maximum number of non-overlapping activities. Explain the greedy strategy, prove it is optimal using an exchange argument, and state the time complexity.
questions.answer:
Greedy strategy: Sort activities by finish time. Select the first activity. For each subsequent activity, select it if its start time >= the finish time of the last selected activity.

Exchange argument proof: Suppose the greedy selects activity G first (earliest finish). Any optimal solution O selects some activity A first. Since G finishes no later than A (G has the earliest finish time), replacing A with G in O leaves all subsequent selections valid (they start after G's finish >= A's finish). The modified solution has the same count, so including G is safe. By induction, the greedy solution is optimal.

Time complexity: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for the greedy scan = O(n log n).

rubric.key: Correct greedy strategy (sort by finish time)
Clear exchange argument showing greedy choice is safe
Correct time complexity with justification
